title: Pele and the Lava
summary: Across the vast, sapphire expanse of the Pacific, where the horizon dissolves into a haze of gold and blue, lies the archipelago of Hawaiʻi—a chain of emerald peaks born from the violent passion of a single deity.
full_text:
Across the vast, sapphire expanse of the Pacific, where the horizon dissolves into a haze of gold and blue, lies the archipelago of Hawaiʻi—a chain of emerald peaks born from the violent passion of a single deity. This is the realm of Pele, the goddess of fire, lightning, wind, and volcanoes. She is not a goddess of gentle warmth, but of creative destruction, a force of nature as unpredictable as the magma that flows from the heart of the earth.

Pele did not originate in Hawaiʻi. She came from the distant lands of Tahiti, sailing in a great canoe carved from the wood of sacred trees. She fled her home, driven by a fierce desire for a place where she could build her fires without restraint, and where her power would not be contested. As she navigated the endless ocean, guided by the stars and the songs of the wind, she sought a peak high enough to reach the heavens, a mountain that would serve as her throne.

When she first reached the islands, Pele found the land cold and the peaks shrouded in snow. She attempted to make her home in the Koʻolaus, but the soil was too damp, the rains too frequent. She moved to Mauna Kea, but the summit was a wasteland of ice and wind. Frustrated and longing for the heat of the deep earth, she traveled south to the island of Hawaiʻi, where she discovered Kīlauea—a mountain that pulsed with a hidden, subterranean energy. Here, the earth was thin, and the fire was waiting.

With the pau, her sacred digging spade given to her by her grandmother, Pele began to carve her kingdom. She dug deep into the crust of the world, releasing the trapped magma in a torrent of liquid gold. The fire roared, the sky turned the color of a bruised plum, and the lava flowed down the slopes in rivers of molten stone. Where the lava met the crashing waves of the Pacific, it hissed and screamed, hardening into black basalt and creating new land. Again and again, Pele expanded her shores, adding layer upon layer of stone until the islands grew and flourished.

But Pele's existence was marked by an eternal conflict. Her elder sister, Namaka, the goddess of the sea, viewed Pele's fire as an intrusion, a scar upon the pristine blue of the ocean. Namaka unleashed the full fury of the tides, sending colossal waves to drown the fires and extinguish the goddess's ambition. The battle between fire and water lasted for eons—a cosmic war of steam and ash. Pele retreated further into the mountains, deeper into the craters, but her fire never truly died. It only grew more concentrated, more potent.

Today, Pele still resides in the Halemaʻumaʻu crater, her presence felt in every tremor of the earth and every plume of sulfuric smoke. She is the goddess of the cycle of life: she destroys the forests and the villages with her lava, but in doing so, she creates the most fertile soil on Earth. The blackened lava fields eventually give way to lush greens and vibrant flowers, a testament to the fact that beauty can only be born from the ashes of destruction.

Those who venture onto her slopes do so with a mixture of awe and terror. They leave offerings of ohelo berries and lei, praying for her favor and begging for her restraint. For to know Pele is to know that nature does not negotiate. She is the flame that consumes and the fire that creates, the eternal heartbeat of the islands, reminding all who dwell there that they live at the mercy of a goddess who breathes fire.

title: The Night Marchers
summary: In Hawaii, when the sun dips below the Pacific and the shadows of the volcanic peaks stretch across the land like dark fingers, the wise ones stay indoors.
full_text:
In Hawaii, when the sun dips below the Pacific and the shadows of the volcanic peaks stretch across the land like dark fingers, the wise ones stay indoors. They know that when the night is quiet and the air grows cold, the Night Marchers walk.

The Night Marchers are the spirits of ancient Hawaiian warriors. They were the chiefs and warriors of old, who fought and died defending their islands. They march in long processions, carrying torches and spears, beating drums and chanting in voices that carry across the valleys. Their path follows the ancient trails — the ala loa — that connected villages before the first ships arrived.

Those who have seen them say the procession is terrifying and beautiful. The warriors are dressed in feather cloaks and helmets, their faces painted for battle. The chiefs are carried on litters, their eyes glowing with the fire of the old gods. At the head of the procession walks a figure carrying a torch that burns with a cold, white flame. This is the marker of the Night Marchers: when you see the flame, you have only moments to act.

Hawaiian tradition teaches that if you hear the conch shell blowing at night, followed by the beat of drums and the chant of warriors, you must fall to the ground and lie flat on your stomach. Do not look up. Do not run. Do not make eye contact with the marchers. The Night Marchers are on their way to a sacred destination, and mortals who meet their gaze are taken with them — their spirits forced to join the endless procession.

But there is one way to be safe. If you are of Hawaiian ancestry, you can call out the name of a chief in the procession, proving your lineage. The marchers will recognize you as family and pass you by. This is why Hawaiians learn their genealogies — they are not just names and dates, but passwords that protect the living from the dead.

The Night Marchers still walk the ancient trails of Hawaii. They are the guardians of the old ways, the echo of a Hawaii that existed before the modern world.
moral: The ancestors still walk among us—respect them.

title: The Menehune Builders
summary: Before the first Polynesian canoes touched the shores of Hawaii, the islands were already inhabited.
full_text:
Before the first Polynesian canoes touched the shores of Hawaii, the islands were already inhabited. The Menehune were there — a race of small, industrious people who worked only at night, building the great structures that still stand today.

The Menehune were no taller than a child's waist, with broad shoulders and strong arms. They had dark skin, large eyes that could see in the dark, and a love of work that bordered on obsession. They could move stones that ten ordinary men could not lift. They could build a fishpond in a single night and a temple before dawn.

According to Hawaiian legend, the Menehune were the original engineers of the islands. They built the Alekoko Fishpond on Kauai, a massive enclosure of lava rock that still holds fish today. They built the Menehune Ditch, a stone-lined water channel that runs for miles across the island's interior. They built the heiaus — temples dedicated to the old gods — on every island.

The Menehune worked only at night and demanded absolute secrecy. If a human watched them work, they would abandon the project forever. There are many stories of curious villagers who hid in the bushes to watch the Menehune, only to see the workers vanish, leaving half-finished walls that stand to this day.

The Menehune were also tricksters. They loved games, riddles, and challenges. They would test humans by asking impossible questions, and those who answered poorly would find their crops ruined or their tools broken. But those who answered wisely would wake to find their houses repaired and their fields cultivated.

Over time, the Menehune grew fewer. Some say they left Hawaii when the modern world arrived, retreating to a hidden valley on Kauai where no human can find them. Others say they are still there, working through the darkest nights, building structures that will last for a thousand years. The Hawaii Department of Transportation once rerouted a road around a Menehune stone wall rather than disturb their work.

title: Degei the Serpent God
summary: The creator god who brought humans from the mountains. Centers on Degei,  from Fiji. deity associated with Polynesian heavens and Polynesian sacred sites..
full_text:
In the beginning, there was Degei. He was a great serpent, coiled in the darkness before the world was born, his body stretching farther than the eye could see. He was the first god of Fiji, the creator who shaped the islands from the sea and breathed life into the first humans.

According to Fijian mythology, Degei created the first man and woman from clay. He molded their bodies with his own coils and gave them the breath of life. They lived in a perfect garden called Burotukula, where the trees bore fruit year-round and the waters were always warm. But the first humans were curious and disobedient. They ate a forbidden fruit and were cast out of the garden.

Degei was heartbroken but not vengeful. He taught them how to fish, how to build canoes, how to read the stars for navigation. He gave them the knowledge of tapa cloth and kava ceremonies. Then he withdrew from the world, curling up in a cave high in the mountains of Viti Levu.

Today, Degei is said to live in a cave on the island of Bau. He is the guardian of Fijian tradition and the judge of human souls. When people die, their spirits travel to the cave, where Degei judges them by their deeds. Those who lived well are allowed to pass into the afterlife. Those who wronged others are devoured by the serpent.

Degei is still worshipped in Fiji. Fishermen leave offerings at his cave. When the ground shakes, the Fijians say Degei is turning in his sleep. When the rain falls, they say he is weeping for the world he created and the people he must judge.
moral: The ancestors remain to guide their descendants.

title: The Ghost Torch
summary: In the islands of Fiji, where the jungle meets the coral reefs and the nights are thick with the songs of insects, the villagers tell a story about a flickering light that appears on the mountain paths after dark.
full_text:
In the islands of Fiji, where the jungle meets the coral reefs and the nights are thick with the songs of insects, the villagers tell a story about a flickering light that appears on the mountain paths after dark. They call it the Ghost Torch — a flame that moves without a hand to carry it, wandering the trails where travelers walk alone.

Long ago, there was a man named Ratu who had wronged his village deeply. He had stolen from the communal stores during a time of famine, hoarding food while children went hungry. When the village elders discovered his crime, they banished him. He was forbidden to walk the paths of his ancestors ever again.

Ratu fled into the mountains, carrying only a torch to light his way. He wandered for days, eaten by guilt and hunger, until he collapsed on a ridge overlooking the sea. As his life faded, his torch fell from his hand — but it did not go out. It continued to burn, held by his restless spirit, searching for a path back to the village he could never enter.

To this day, the Ghost Torch appears on the mountain paths of Viti Levu. Travelers who see it say it moves in a straight line, never flickering, never dimming, never stopping. Those who follow it say it leads them safely through the darkest parts of the forest. But those who try to catch it find themselves lost, wandering in circles until dawn.

The Fijian elders say the Ghost Torch is a reminder that even the greatest sin does not erase the love someone holds for their homeland. Ratu's torch still burns because his heart still longs for home.
moral: Help comes to those who need, not those who want.

title: Rangi and Papa: The Separation of Sky and Earth
summary: In the beginning, Ranginui (Sky Father) and Papatuanuku (Earth Mother) lay locked in a tight embrace, leaving their children trapped in darkness between them. The children grew weary of the cramped darkness and debated how to gain freedom. Tane Mahuta, god of forests, proposed separating their parents. While Tawhirimatea opposed, Tane lay on his back and pushed upward with his feet, forcing Rangi upward and thrusting Papa down. Thus light entered the world. Tawhirimatea, enraged, joined his father in the sky and sent storms against his siblings, beginning the eternal conflict between wind, forest, sea, and land.

title: Maui Fishing Up the North Island
summary: Maui's brothers refused to take him fishing, so he hid in their canoe and revealed himself at sea. Using a magical fishhook made from the jawbone of his ancestor Murirangawhenua, and chanting powerful incantations, Maui caught a gigantic fish. He instructed his brothers to wait until he had appeased Tangaroa before carving the fish, but they grew impatient and began cutting. The fish thrashed, forming the mountains, valleys, lakes, and rugged coastline of Te Ika-a-Maui (the North Island of New Zealand). Maui's canoe became the South Island, and Stewart Island became his anchor stone.

title: Maui Slowing the Sun
summary: Maui observed that the sun, Tamanuitera, moved across the sky too quickly, leaving insufficient daylight for working, cooking, and fishing. He convinced his brothers to help capture the sun and force it to slow down. They traveled east to the pit where the sun rose, built a wall of clay to hide behind, and wove strong ropes from flax. When the sun rose, Maui chanted a karakia and the brothers threw ropes around the sun's body, trapping it. Maui struck the sun with his magic jawbone until it agreed to travel more slowly across the sky, giving humanity longer days for their labors.

title: Maui Stealing Fire from Mahuika
summary: After all fires in Hawaiki had been extinguished, Maui volunteered to visit his ancestress Mahuika, the goddess of fire, to obtain new flames. She pulled out one of her burning fingernails and gave it to him. However, Maui's trickster nature took over and he extinguished it, then returned for another. He repeated this deception until Mahuika had given away all but her last fingernail. Enraged, she threw her final nail at Maui, igniting a great forest fire. Maui transformed into a hawk to escape, then called upon his thunder god ancestors to send rain. Mahuika saved a few sparks which she threw into specific trees, explaining why certain woods are best for fire-making.

title: Maui and Hine-nui-te-po: The Quest for Immortality
summary: Maui sought to conquer death itself by destroying Hine-nui-te-po, the goddess of death. He knew that if he could pass through her body unharmed, humanity would gain immortality. He took the smallest forest birds as his companions and found the goddess sleeping. Maui instructed the birds to remain silent while he crawled into her mouth and through her body. He warned them not to laugh. However, when only his legs remained visible, the tiny Tiwakawaka (fantail) could not contain its amusement at the sight. Its laughter woke Hine-nui-te-po, who snapped her obsidian teeth shut, cutting Maui in half. Thus Maui died and death became permanent for all humankind.

title: Kupe Discovering Aotearoa
summary: Kupe, a great Polynesian navigator and chief from Hawaiki, pursued the giant octopus Te Wheke-a-Muturangi across the Pacific Ocean after it stole his bait and caused chaos. His wife Hine-te-Aparangi sighted land and named it Aotearoa (Land of the Long White Cloud). Kupe and his crew landed at the Hokianga Harbour, where he battled and killed the giant octopus. He explored the coastline, named many landmarks, and returned to Hawaiki with tales of the new land, providing the first detailed navigational knowledge that later guided the great migration of Maori to New Zealand.

title: Ngake and Whataitai: The Taniwha of Wellington Harbour
summary: The great taniwha Ngake and Whataitai lived in Lake Rotoroa (now Wellington Harbour) when it was still an enclosed lake. Ngake heard tales of the vast ocean beyond the hills and grew restless. He threw his body against the land at the southern end, eventually breaking through at what is now the harbor entrance, creating Te Whanganui-a-Tara. Whataitai tried to follow but became trapped in shallow waters as the lake drained. He transformed into a hill, Mt. Victoria, where his spirit still watches over the harbor. The story explains the geological formation of Wellington Harbour and the Miramar Peninsula.

title: Hatupatu and the Bird-Woman
summary: Hatupatu was a young Maori warrior who was captured by Kurangaituku, a fearsome bird-woman with sharp claws and a taste for human flesh. She kept him in her cave dwelling, but clever Hatupatu bided his time. When Kurangaituku relaxed her guard, he escaped, running across the rugged landscape of the central North Island. The bird-woman pursued relentlessly. Hatupatu called upon the gods for help and was saved by the hot springs and geysers of Rotorua, where the scalding waters repelled his pursuer. He hid in a narrow rock crevice that Kurangaituku could not reach, and she eventually gave up the chase.

title: Hinemoa and Tutanekai
summary: Hinemoa, a high-born maiden from the shores of Lake Rotorua, fell in love with Tutanekai, a younger son of a chief who lived on Mokoia Island. Her family disapproved of the match. Tutanekai would play his flute across the lake each night, and Hinemoa listened from the shore. One night, spurred by her love, Hinemoa tied gourds to her body for buoyancy and swam across the cold lake in darkness. She reached Mokoia Island and found her way to Tutanekai by following his music. The lovers were reunited, and her family eventually accepted their union. This story is one of the most celebrated Maori love legends.

title: The Battle of the Mountains
summary: The mountains of the central North Island were once living warriors who fought for the love of the beautiful mountain Pihanga. Tongariro, the greatest volcano, won her heart. In jealousy, Taranaki (then located in the center) challenged Tongariro. During their terrible battle, Tongariro erupted in fury and Taranaki was defeated. Wounded and exiled, Taranaki carved a great path as he moved westward, forming the Whanganui River. He now stands alone near the west coast, his peak forever turned away from the central plateau where Pihanga lies. The story also includes Ruapehu and Ngauruhoe, Tongariro's faithful allies.

title: Tangaroa and Tane: The War of Forest and Sea
summary: After the separation of Rangi and Papa, furious Tawhirimatea drove his brother Tangaroa, god of the sea, into the ocean depths. Tangaroa's descendants included Ikatere (fish) and Tu-te-wehiwehi (reptiles). When Tane Mahuta, god of the forest, offered shelter to Tu-te-wehiwehi and his reptile children, they abandoned the sea for the land. This betrayal enraged Tangaroa, who declared eternal war on Tane. To this day, the sea consumes the shores and forests, while Tane's creatures hide from the ocean. Fish and reptiles were divided—fish remained loyal to Tangaroa while reptiles joined Tane on land, creating the lasting enmity.

title: Pele's Journey from Kahiki to Hawaii
summary: Pele, the volcano goddess, was born in Tahiti (Kahiki) to Haumea and Kane Milohai. Banished by her father because of her fierce temper, Pele sailed with her brothers in a great canoe across the Pacific. She dug fire pits at each island she visited, creating Diamond Head on Oahu and Haleakala on Maui. On Maui, her sister Namakaokahai (the sea goddess) attacked her with massive waves. Pele was torn apart, but her spirit fled to the Big Island where she dug the deep pit of Kilauea. There she established her permanent home at Halemaumau Crater, goddess of volcanoes whose fiery breath shapes the Hawaiian landscape.

title: Hi'iaka's Journey to Bring Lohiau to Pele
summary: Pele fell in love with Lohiau, a chief of Kauai, during a spirit dream. She sent her beloved youngest sister Hi'iaka to retrieve him. Hi'iaka agreed on the condition that Pele protect her sacred lehua groves and friend Hopoe. On Kauai, Hi'iaka found Lohiau had died of heartbreak and spent thirty days chanting to revive him. On the journey back, Pele grew jealous of Hi'iaka's prolonged absence. In rage, Pele destroyed Hi'iaka's lehua groves and encased Hopoe in lava. Hi'iaka, seeing this through far-sight, defiantly embraced Lohiau at the crater's edge. Pele engulfed them in flames. Hi'iaka revived Lohiau again, and ultimately Pele allowed them to return to Kauai together.

title: Pele and Kamapua'a
summary: The volcano goddess Pele and Kamapua'a, the pig demigod, had a tumultuous relationship oscillating between love and war. Kamapua'a could transform between human and giant pig forms and commanded the rains and forests. When he pursued Pele, she refused him, and a great battle erupted across the Big Island. Pele sent rivers of lava at him while Kamapua'a countered with floods and rain, extinguishing her fires. Neither could defeat the other. In some versions, they eventually married and had a child. Their union represents the eternal balance between fire and water, volcanic destruction and forest regeneration—the fundamental forces that create and renew the Hawaiian landscape.

title: The Kumulipo: Hawaiian Creation Chant
summary: The Kumulipo is the sacred Hawaiian creation chant, a genealogical epic tracing the universe from its primordial origins. It begins in total darkness (Po) with the birth of coral, seaweed, and the first sea creatures. The chant follows the emergence of life from ocean to land, from the humblest plants to animals and finally humans. The chant connects the Hawaiian chiefly lineage directly to the gods, establishing mana through unbroken descent from the primal darkness. The Kumulipo was famously chanted in full at the birth of Kamehameha the Great, and later translated by Queen Liliuokalani. It stands as one of the greatest surviving works of Polynesian oral literature.

title: Poliahu and Pele: The Snow Goddess Rivalry
summary: Poliahu, the lovely snow goddess who drapes Mauna Kea in white, and Pele, the volcano goddess of Kilauea, were rivals for the affections of a handsome chief. Poliahu was renowned for her beauty and cool temperament, while Pele was passionate and jealous. During a gathering of goddesses, Pele challenged Poliahu and ridiculed her. In anger, Poliahu revealed her true divine nature, covering the mountain in blinding snow. The two goddesses have contested the Big Island ever since—Pele heating it from below with lava, Poliahu cooling it from above with snow. Their eternal struggle shapes Hawaii's landscape through volcanic eruptions and winter snows.

title: The Menehune: Hawaii's Hidden Builders
summary: The Menehune are a legendary race of small but powerful people who inhabited Hawaii before the Polynesian voyagers arrived. They were master builders who worked only at night, constructing fishponds, temples (heiau), and irrigation systems across the islands. The Menehune could complete in one night what would take ordinary humans months. The Alekoko Fishpond on Kauai is said to be their work, built in a single night by a line of Menehune stretching from the mountains to the sea. If a human watched them work, the Menehune would stop and vanish. Today, the word Menehune is used to describe any mysterious or unexplained construction in the islands.

title: The Night Marchers of Hawaii
summary: The Huaka'i Po (Night Marchers) are ghostly processions of ancient Hawaiian warrior spirits who march along traditional pathways on moonlit nights. Led by fire-wielding priests and accompanied by the sound of conch shells and war chants, they travel to sacred sites and battlefields. They can appear as torches floating in the darkness, with only the sound of marching feet. According to legend, anyone who looks directly at the Night Marchers will be killed, but those who lie face down and show proper respect will be spared. The procession includes the spirits of great chiefs, alii, carrying their divine mana through the landscapes they once ruled.

title: Tagaloa Creates the Samoan Islands
summary: In the beginning, only Tagaloa existed in the heavens above the endless waters. He looked down and decided to create a place to rest. He formed a great rock called Manu'atele, then divided it into pieces that became the stepping stones of the Pacific. The largest pieces became the islands of Savai'i and Upolu, while smaller ones scattered to form Tonga, Fiji, and other islands. Tagaloa then created the land, the forests, and the people, establishing the fa'a Samoa (Samoan way) of chieftainship, social order, and spiritual devotion. He sent his son to rule the earth, and from this lineage came the noble families of Samoa.

title: Sina and the Eel (Origin of the Coconut)
summary: The beautiful maiden Sina drew water from a pool where she discovered a young eel. She took it home as a pet and named it Tuna. The eel grew rapidly, outgrowing bowls, then pools, and becoming unnaturally large. Wherever Sina bathed, the eel appeared and encircled her. Frightened, she fled across multiple islands, but the eel always found her. Finally, the eel revealed itself as the King of Fiji transformed by magic, who confessed his love and asked Sina to bury his head. Though shocked, Sina complied. From the buried head grew the first coconut tree, its three dark pores representing the eel's eyes and mouth. When cracked, the coconut's shell resembles the eel's face.

title: Nafanua: The Warrior Goddess
summary: Nafanua, daughter of Saveasi'uleo (god of Pulotu) and Tilafaiga, was born a female but hidden in the forest by her mother out of shame. She emerged as a fierce warrior when Samoa was oppressed by tyrannical rulers. Disguising her chest so enemies would not know she was a woman, Nafanua led rebellions that freed the people of Savai'i from bondage. She was relentless and merciful only in victory, bringing peace after winning the wars between the eastern and western districts. Nafanua established the matai (chiefly) system and is revered as Samoa's greatest war goddess. After her earthly exploits, she returned to Pulotu, the spirit underworld, and her legacy endures in Samoan ceremonial protocol.

title: Taema and Tilafaiga: The Origin of Tattooing
summary: The twin sisters Taema and Tilafaiga, divine daughters of the god Tagaloa, journeyed from Fiji to Samoa carrying the sacred art of tattooing. They swam across the ocean holding a basket containing the tools and patterns for tatau. As they approached the Samoan shore, they began singing 'Fafafofoga o le tama' ('The tattoo fits the man'), establishing that body art was meant for men, not women. The sisters taught the Samoan people the intricate patterns, spiritual protocols, and practical techniques of traditional tattooing. Their gift of the pe'a (male tattoo covering from waist to knees) and malu (female leg tattoo) became a central pillar of Samoan cultural identity and rite of passage.

title: Moso's Footprint
summary: The giant Moso was one of the first humans created by Tagaloa on the island of Savai'i. His enormous footprint can still be seen pressed deep into solid lava rock at the village of Salelologa. According to legend, Moso stood so tall that he could step from one island to another. His footprint was left when he stood on Savai'i and pushed the island of Upolu away to create the Apolima Strait between them. Moso also owned a legendary war club that could kill hundreds with a single swing. The footprint remains a sacred site, and the story explains the geographic separation between the two main Samoan islands while affirming the power of the original human inhabitants.

title: Hikule'o, Tangaloa, and Maui Divide the Cosmos
summary: In Tongan creation myth, after the primordial god Taufulifonua fathered Hikule'o with his sister Havea Lolofonua, the three great powers divided the universe. Hikule'o received Pulotu, the underworld realm of spirits and the afterlife. Tangaloa took the vast sky and heavens, becoming ruler of the celestial domain. Maui received the underworld (the earth's foundation) and was tasked with fishing up the islands from the ocean. This tripartite division established the cosmic order—the sky above (Tangaloa's realm), the earth's surface (under Maui's influence), and the underworld of Pulotu (Hikule'o's domain where souls journey after death). The balance between these three realms governs Tongan spiritual beliefs.

title: The Origin of Kava
summary: A Tongan chief and his wife prayed to the gods for a child. Their prayers were answered with a son, but he was born with leprosy. The grief-stricken parents buried the child, and from his grave grew two plants—a tall, branching plant (the kava) and a shorter broad-leafed plant (the sugarcane). A wandering spirit discovered these plants and instructed the people on how to prepare them. The kava root was chewed and mixed with water to create a ceremonial drink that brought peace, unity, and connection to the ancestors. Thus kava became the sacred beverage of Tongan ceremony, used to seal agreements, welcome visitors, and commune with the gods.

title: Ahoeitu and the Founding of the Tupou Dynasty
summary: Ahoeitu was born to a Tongan chiefess and the god Eitumatupua, who descended from heaven on a great tree. The jealous half-brothers of Ahoeitu conspired against him, murdering him and cutting his body into pieces. When Eitumatupua discovered the crime, he restored Ahoeitu to life and punished the treacherous brothers. Ahoeitu became the first of the Tu'i Tonga line, establishing the sacred dynasty that ruled Tonga for over a thousand years. His descendants held supreme temporal and spiritual authority, their mana legitimized by direct divine ancestry. The story reinforces the sacred nature of Tongan kingship and the consequences of defying divinely ordained rule.

title: Maui Pulling Up the Tongan Islands
summary: Maui Kisikisi (Little Maui) sat in his canoe fishing but catching nothing. Growing frustrated, he reached down through the ocean with his powerful arms and grasped the sea floor. With tremendous effort, he pulled upward, bringing the Tongan islands to the surface. As he hauled, the coral, sand, and rock emerged from the depths, forming the archipelago. The story explains why Tonga's islands are relatively flat compared to volcanic islands—they were pulled up, not thrown up by volcanic force. This tale is part of a regional Maui cycle, adapted to explain Tonga's unique geography of low coral atolls and raised limestone islands.

title: Ta'aroa Creates the World from His Shell
summary: In the beginning, Ta'aroa, the supreme creator god of Tahiti, existed alone inside a great shell called Rumia, spinning through the endless void. Bored with his solitude, he shook himself and cracked the shell open. He threw pieces of shell outward to form the rocks, sand, and foundation of the world. From his backbone he shaped the mountains; snip from his tears he filled the oceans, lakes, and rivers. His scales became the fish and turtles, his blood colored the rainbow, and his feathers became the trees and bushes. Ta'aroa then created Tane, Ru, and Hina, who helped him decorate the sky with stars, hang the sun and moon, and populate the earth with humanity.

title: Hiro the Trickster and the Magic Canoe
summary: Hiro, the Tahitian trickster god of thieves and the son of the god Ta'aroa, built a magical canoe that could sail faster than the wind. Using his cunning and his enchanted vessel, Hiro traveled across the Society Islands performing daring thefts and outsmarting chiefs and gods alike. He stole fish from the markets, food from the temples, and once even attempted to steal the island of Bora Bora itself. His exploits angered the other gods, who eventually unleashed a great storm to stop him. The story of Hiro's rebellion and eventual downfall teaches that even the cleverest trickster cannot escape divine justice, but his resourcefulness remains celebrated in Tahitian storytelling.

title: Oro: God of War and the Arioi Society
summary: Oro, son of Ta'aroa, was the Tahitian god of war and peace. He descended to earth on a rainbow and took a mortal woman as his wife, establishing the Arioi—a secret society of warriors, artists, and priests who spread his cult throughout the Society Islands. The Arioi traveled between islands performing sacred dances, chants, and dramatic performances honoring Oro. They had elaborate initiation grades, and their ceremonies involved ritual drama, feasting, and human sacrifice. Oro became the most powerful deity of Tahiti, displacing Tane as the chief god. His cult reached its height at the marae of Taputapuatea on Raiatea, the spiritual center of Eastern Polynesia.

title: The Origin of Breadfruit
summary: During a terrible famine in Tahiti, the people prayed to the gods for deliverance. The god Rongo, moved by their suffering, transformed himself into a great breadfruit tree. The tree bore fruit that tasted like fresh bread when roasted, saving the people from starvation. Each part of the tree proved useful—the fruit as food, the sap as glue and medicine, the leaves as plates, and the wood for building canoes. Rongo also taught the people how to cultivate the tree through cuttings rather than seeds, ensuring the breadfruit would follow them wherever they voyaged. The story explains why breadfruit was carried by Polynesian settlers across the Pacific and remains a staple food across the islands.

title: Makemake and the Birdman Cult
summary: Makemake, the supreme creator god of Rapa Nui, shaped the world and gave life to the island. He created the birds, the fish, and the people, then withdrew to watch over them from the heavens. As Rapa Nui's society evolved, Makemake became associated with the Tangata Manu (Birdman) cult centered at the ceremonial village of Orongo. Each year, representatives of competing clans would descend the sheer cliffs, swim across shark-infested waters to the islet of Motu Nui, and retrieve the first Manutara (sooty tern) egg of the season. The successful competitor became the Tangata Manu, the Birdman of the year, gaining supreme temporal power for the tribe.

title: Hotu Matu'a Discovers Rapa Nui
summary: King Hotu Matu'a lived in the mythical land of Hiva, which was sinking beneath the ocean. A prophet named Hau Maka appeared in Hotu Matu'a's dream and told him to search for a new island called Rapa Nui. Seven explorers were sent ahead in a canoe, finding the uninhabited island. They returned to report its location. Hotu Matu'a then sailed with two large canoes carrying approximately a hundred people, including his wife, children, and essential plants and animals. When they landed at Anakena Beach, Hotu Matu'a established the first settlement and divided the island among his descendants. His nine sons became the ancestors of the Rapa Nui clans, and he inaugurated the moai carving tradition to honor his lineage.

title: The Aku-Aku Spirits of Rapa Nui
summary: The Aku-Aku are the powerful ancestral spirits of Rapa Nui, believed to inhabit the island's caves, cliffs, and sacred sites. These spirits could be both protectors and tricksters, guiding the living toward prosperity or leading them astray. The Aku-Aku were deeply connected to the moai statues—each moai embodied an ancestor's spirit, channeling their mana to protect the community. Oral traditions tell of spirit encounters in caves, where Aku-Aku would offer knowledge in exchange for offerings. The most powerful Aku-Aku was Ure, who taught the islanders the art of moai carving. Belief in Aku-Aku persists today, and many Rapanui still avoid certain caves and areas believed to be spirit dwellings.

title: How the Moai Walked
summary: According to Rapanui oral tradition, the great stone moai statues were carved at the Rano Raraku quarry and then walked to their final ahu (ceremonial platforms) across the island. The moai walked at night, powered by mana from the ancestors channeled through the kahuna (priests). Using a system of ropes and rocking motions, teams would chant and coordinate to make the massive statues 'walk' upright, step by step, sometimes for miles. The largest moai, Paro, walked over 10 kilometers before taking its position at Ahu Te Pito Kura. One legend describes how a moai stopped mid-journey when the villagers broke a sacred tapu, and it remains fallen to this day, frozen by the broken taboo.

title: Tangaroa Creation Myth (Cook Islands)
summary: In the Cook Islands version of creation, Vari-ma-te-takere (the primordial mother) lived confined in a narrow space within Avaiki. She plucked pieces from her right side and left side to create six children. The first was Avatea (Vatea), a moon god with the right half of a man and left half of a fish. Avatea married Papa, and they bore twin sons: Tangaroa, god of the sea, and Rongo, god of agriculture. Tangaroa and Rongo shared dominion over all things—with Tangaroa taking everything red. Rangi, grandson of Rongo, later pulled Mangaia island up from Avaiki and became its first king, establishing the royal lineage. This creation cycle forms the foundation of Cook Islands cosmology.

title: Ina and the Shark
summary: The beautiful maiden Ina longed to reach the island of her lover Tinirau, the god of the ocean. A great shark offered to carry her across the sea. During the journey, Ina grew thirsty and cracked open a coconut, drinking its milk. Later, she struck a second coconut directly onto the shark's head to open it, hurting the shark badly. The shark tried to shake her off, but Tekea the Great, king of all sharks, rose from the deep and carried her safely to Tinirau's island. The dent on the shark's head from Ina's coconut blow remains visible to this day, and the story explains why sharks bear a distinctive indentation on their heads.

title: Tangiia and Karika Divide Rarotonga
summary: Two great warrior chiefs, Tangiia and Karika, both arrived at Rarotonga claiming ownership of the island. They fought fiercely, neither able to defeat the other. Realizing their conflict would destroy the land they sought to rule, they agreed to negotiate. Tangiia and Karika divided Rarotonga between them, establishing boundaries through a sacred covenant. Tangiia took the northern and eastern districts while Karika claimed the south and west. Their treaty established the political structure of the island, and their descendants became the founding lines of the major Rarotongan tribes. The division they set has shaped Cook Islands society for generations.

title: The Marquesan Hero Nukuhiva
summary: The legendary hero Nukuhiva emerged from the sacred valley of Taipivai and united the warring tribes of the Marquesas Islands. Gifted by the gods with extraordinary strength, courage, and wisdom, Nukuhiva traveled across Nuku Hiva, Hiva Oa, and Ua Pou, establishing peace through a combination of diplomacy and martial prowess. He codified the tapu system, defined territorial boundaries, and instituted the feast cycles that governed Marquesan society. His descendants became the island chiefs who maintained his laws long after his death. The story of Nukuhiva embodies the Marquesan values of warrior honor, tribal unity, and reverence for ancestral tradition.

title: Tuamotu: The Voyage of Kiwa
summary: The great navigator Kiwa sailed across the Pacific guided by the stars, discovering the Tuamotu Archipelago. According to Tuamotuan tradition, Kiwa was sent by the gods to find new lands for the growing population of the central islands. He followed the flight paths of seabirds and the patterns of ocean swells to locate the low coral atolls hidden on the horizon. At each island, Kiwa planted coconuts, released breeding pairs of fish into the lagoons, and established the tapu that would protect the fragile atoll ecosystems. His voyages mapped the Tuamotu chain, and the navigational knowledge he passed down—star paths, wave patterns, and bird signs—remained the foundation of Tuamotuan seafaring for generations.

title: Hina: The Woman in the Moon
summary: Hina (also called Sina or Ina across different Polynesian cultures) was a mortal woman who became the moon goddess. In the Maori version, Hina went to fetch water at night and cursed the moon when she stumbled in darkness. The moon descended, caught her, and lifted her into the sky. In Hawaiian tradition, Hina was the mother of Maui who escaped human troubles and found refuge in the moon. Cook Islands stories tell that the moon god Marama fell in love with Ina and carried her away. In Samoa, Hina makes tapa cloth in the moon, her form visible during full moon nights. Across Polynesia, Hina represents the divine feminine, the cycle of death and rebirth, and the celestial light that guides voyagers at night.

title: Degei the Serpent God and the Creation of Fiji
summary: Degei, the supreme god of Fijian mythology, is a great serpent who created the world, fruits, and the first humans. He hatched an egg from which the first human couple emerged. Degei lives in a cave near the summit of Mount Uluda in the Nakauvandra Range. Earthquakes and thunder are caused by his uneasy turnings within the cave. He judges newly dead souls who pass through one of two caves, Cibaciba or Drakulu, sending a few to paradise (Burotu) and casting most into a lake to sink to Murimuria for reward or punishment. He is also a god of wrath who punishes by destroying crops or sending floods, and is connected to the snake cult throughout Fiji.

title: Dakuwaqa the Shark God
summary: Dakuwaqa is a fearsome shark deity in Fijian mythology who appears as a fierce sea monster guarding the islands. He was greatly respected by fishermen for protecting them from danger at sea. In one creation myth, Dakuwaqa attempted to conquer Kadavu Island by swimming up a river, but the goddess Rokobakaniceva challenged him in the form of a great octopus. After a titanic battle, the octopus pulled out Dakuwaqa's teeth with her eight arms, forcing the shark god to promise never to attack Kadavu again. He became the protector of Kadavu and can shapeshift into any form, but his true form is a muscular Fijian man with the upper torso of a shark.

title: The Origin of Kava in Fiji
summary: In Fijian oral tradition, the origin of kava (yaqona) is tied to a sacred ritual. According to legend, a chief and his followers were instructed by the gods to perform a ceremonial planting. From a specific plant, the roots were discovered to produce a potent drink that connected the drinker to the spirit world. The preparation and drinking of kava became central to Fijian ceremony, used to honor ancestors, settle disputes, and communicate with deities like Degei. The kava ceremony remains the most important cultural ritual in Fiji, with strict protocols governing its preparation, serving, and consumption as a bridge between the human and spiritual realms.

title: To-Kabinana and To-Karvuvu: The Wise and Foolish Brothers
summary: In the mythology of New Britain (Papua New Guinea), the two culture hero brothers To-Kabinana (wise) and To-Karvuvu (foolish) were created when an old woman scratched her arms with pandanus strips while wading in the sea, and the two bloodied strips swelled into boys. To-Kabinana created good things for humanity while To-Karvuvu's attempts went wrong, bringing evil and harmful things into the world, including cannibalism. To-Kabinana climbed a coconut tree and threw two unripe coconuts to the ground, which burst and became his wives. When To-Karvuvu tried to imitate this he threw the nuts point-downward, creating women with flat ugly noses. Their stories explain the origin of many of the world's imperfections.

title: Qat the Creator and the Birth of Night
summary: In the Banks Islands of Vanuatu, the principal god Qat emerged from a stone that exploded as his mother. He had eleven brothers called Tagaro and a companion named Marawa the spider. Qat carved the first three pairs of humans from dracaena wood and brought them to life by playing drums, making them dance. When his brothers tired of constant daylight, Qat traveled to the Torres Islands to purchase Night from I Qong. He brought back Night, a rooster, and an obsidian stone. His brothers lay down and slept while the sky darkened. After a while Qat had the rooster crow to wake them and sliced the dark sky apart with the obsidian, creating the first Dawn. Marawa, envious of Qat's creations, made his own figures and buried them, thus introducing death.

title: Qat and the Sky Maidens
summary: One day Qat discovered a group of sky maidens who had removed their wings to bathe. He quickly buried one pair of wings so that one maiden, Vinmara, had to remain behind. She became his wife and was called Ro-Lei. They lived happily until Qat's mother reproached her daughter-in-law and made her cry. Ro-Lei's tears washed away the earth covering her wings; snip she put them on and flew back to the sky. Qat shot an arrow with a rope tied into the roots of a great banyan tree in the sky and climbed after her. As he climbed back down with Ro-Lei, the root snapped and he plunged to his death while she flew safely away. In other versions, Qat does not die but sails away in a canoe, promising to return one day.

title: The Land-Diving Origin of Pentecost Island
summary: On Pentecost Island in Vanuatu, the tradition of land-diving (naghol) originated from a legend about a woman named Tamalie who was mistreated by her husband. She fled into the forest and climbed a tall banyan tree. When her husband followed, she tied liana vines to her ankles and jumped, surviving the fall. Her husband, thinking he could do the same, jumped without the vines and died. The ritual evolved into a test of courage and a rite of passage for young men, who dive from tall wooden towers with vines tied to their ankles, their hair brushing the earth. The land-diving ceremony is performed annually to ensure a good yam harvest and is believed to have inspired modern bungee jumping.

title: Kamata the Culture Hero of Vanuatu
summary: Kamata is a culture hero in Vanuatu mythology, particularly among the people of Ambrym and Malekula. He is credited with bringing fire, agriculture, and essential knowledge to humanity. Kamata journeyed across the islands performing great deeds, shaping the landscape, and teaching people how to cultivate yams, build houses, and conduct proper ceremonies. He is associated with the volcano spirits of Ambrym, a region known for its active volcanic activity. Kamata's exploits often involve tricking dangerous spirits and defeating monstrous beings. His stories are part of the larger cycle of Melanesian culture heroes who brought civilization to humanity and established social customs that continue to be observed.

title: The Origin of Death in Ambrym
summary: On the island of Ambrym in Vanuatu, a myth explains how death came into the world. After the creation of humanity, the good deities proposed that humans should shed their skin like snakes, preserving their youthful appearance forever and living eternally. However, the evil deities disagreed, arguing that when humans grew too old, they should be buried in the ground forever. Because the one who speaks last prevails in the councils of the gods, the evil deities had the final word, and death became the fate of all humanity. This story belongs to a widespread Melanesian mythological theme explaining why humans die while snakes shed their skins and appear renewed.

title: Adaro: The Sea Spirits of the Solomon Islands
summary: In the mythology of Makira (San Cristobal) in the Solomon Islands, the Adaro are powerful sea spirits that dwell in the ocean. They are described as half-human, half-fish beings who can travel along rainbows and sunlight beams. Adaro are said to have gills on their bodies and fins on their arms and legs. They can be both benevolent and malevolent, sometimes helping fishermen by guiding them to abundant fishing grounds, and other times causing shipwrecks and drowning. The Adaro are closely associated with the shark cults of the Solomon Islands, particularly on Malaita where sharks are considered sacred manifestations of ancestral spirits. Offerings were made to Adaro before long sea voyages to ensure safe passage.

title: The Kakamora: Elves of the Solomon Islands
summary: The Kakamora are mythical elf-like creatures in the folklore of Makira and Guadalcanal in the Solomon Islands. They are described as small, hairy beings with great strength, living in the deep forests and remote mountains. The Kakamora are known to be shy but mischievous, sometimes stealing food from villages or playing tricks on travelers. In some traditions they are considered forest guardians who protect sacred groves and punish those who disrespect nature. Their small size allows them to move through the forest undetected. Stories about the Kakamora serve as cautionary tales, warning children and adults alike to respect the boundaries between human settlements and the wild spirit-inhabited forests of the Solomon Islands.

title: Kahausibware: The Creator Goddess
summary: Kahausibware is a creator goddess in the mythology of the Mono-Alu people of the Solomon Islands. She is a supreme female deity credited with creating the world, the islands, and all living things. In the beginning, Kahausibware created the land by pulling islands up from the sea. She then fashioned humans from clay and breathed life into them, teaching them how to build canoes, plant gardens, and fish. As a creator goddess, she represents the feminine creative principle and is associated with fertility, growth, and the abundance of the sea and forest. Her worship was central to Mono-Alu religious life, with offerings made to ensure good harvests and successful fishing expeditions.

title: Kilibob and Manup: The Two Brothers of Papua New Guinea
summary: In the mythology of northeastern Papua New Guinea, Kilibob and Manup are mythical brothers whose travels created the islands and peoples of the region. Kilibob traveled by canoe along the northern coast, creating islands and bestowing gifts like fishing nets, canoes, and knowledge of navigation. Manup followed a southern route and brought agriculture, domesticated animals, and ceremonial practices. Wherever they landed, the brothers left behind different languages and customs. Their journeys explain the cultural diversity of Papua New Guinea's coastal peoples. The brothers eventually became rivals, and their conflict is said to have created the distinct identities of the different language groups, as each brother favored different communities with different skills and knowledge.

title: Hatuibwari: The Dragon God of the Solomon Islands
summary: Hatuibwari is a powerful dragon-like being from the mythology of the Solomon Islands, particularly among the people of Guadalcanal. Described as a massive serpent with the features of a dragon, Hatuibwari is a creator and cultural hero who shaped the islands and taught humans essential skills. In some accounts, Hatuibwari is a dual-sex being who created the first humans from clay and gave them fire, tools, and knowledge of agriculture. The being is associated with both creation and destruction, capable of bringing rain and fertility but also earthquakes and storms when angered. Hatuibwari's serpentine nature connects it to the widespread Melanesian motif of powerful snake deities who play central roles in creation myths across the region.

title: The Origin of Fire (Motu People)
summary: Among the Motu people of southern Papua New Guinea, a myth tells that the ancestors originally ate all food raw because they had no fire. One day, smoke was seen rising far out at sea. A dog volunteered to investigate the source, swimming across the water. The dog reached an island where women were cooking with fire. Seizing a blazing brand in its mouth, the dog swam safely back to the mainland, bringing fire to the people. Another version from the Massim people of southeastern Papua tells of an old woman named Goga who possessed fire from her own body but kept it for herself, eating cooked food while serving raw food to others. When a man discovered her secret and stole a firebrand, the fire spread rapidly, eventually reaching a tree where a snake lived, setting its tail permanently ablaze as the source of fire for humanity.

title: The Old Woman and the Origin of the Sea
summary: Among the Baining people of New Britain, Papua New Guinea, a myth explains how the sea was created. In the beginning, the sea was only a tiny waterhole from which an old woman extracted saltwater to flavor her food, keeping it hidden under a cover of tapa cloth. One day, her sons became curious and tore open the cover. The farther they tore, the larger the waterhole grew. Terrified by the expanding water, they fled, each carrying a corner of the cloth, which caused the water to spread until it became the vast ocean. In desperation, the old woman hastily planted twigs along the edge of the shore, creating the first coastline and preventing the ocean from destroying all things. This story is found in variants throughout Melanesia.

title: Abaia the Magical Eel
summary: Abaia is a huge, magical eel from the mythology of Fiji, the Solomon Islands, and Vanuatu. This enormous freshwater eel is said to dwell at the bottom of deep lakes and rivers, acting as a guardian spirit of the waters. In Fijian tradition, Abaia is associated with Degei and is considered a powerful spirit being that can control fish and water creatures. Fishermen who respect Abaia and offer proper rituals are blessed with abundant catches, while those who disrespect the waters face misfortune. Some stories describe Abaia creating lakes by thrashing its enormous body, forming depressions that filled with water. The eel is a totemic animal for some clans, and killing a large eel is considered taboo as it may be an incarnation of Abaia.

title: Tagaro and Suqe-Matua: The Creation of Good and Evil
summary: On Ambae Island in Vanuatu, the culture hero Tagaro is a creator figure pitted against his twelfth brother Suqe-matua. Tagaro and Suqe-matua shared the work of creation, but whatever Suqe-matua did was wrong. They always disagreed, but the word of Tagaro always prevailed. Tagaro created useful plants, gentle animals, and beneficial customs, while Suqe-matua created weeds, dangerous creatures, and harmful practices. Tagaro made ten human figures from mud and breathed life into them. He then threw a fruit at one of the figures, transforming the man into a woman, thus establishing the two sexes. The Tagaro figure is related to the Polynesian deity Tangaroa, showing the cultural connections across the Pacific.

title: Areop-Enap: The Spider Who Created the World
summary: In Nauruan creation mythology, the primordial being was Areop-Enap, an ancient spider who existed alone in darkness on an endless sea. Searching for food, Areop-Enap found an enormous clam that swallowed him. Inside the darkness, he discovered two snails. He slept three days transferring his power to each snail, then sent the smaller snail to open the clam, leaving a phosphorescent trail. In the light, he found a white worm called Rigi. Areop-Enap cast a strength spell on Rigi to pry open the clam. The clam died from the effort, and Areop-Enap made the lower shell into Earth and the upper into the sky. He placed the smaller snail in the west to become the Moon and the larger snail in the east to become the Sun. Rigi drowned in his own sweat and was hung in the sky as the Milky Way. Humans were created from stones to support the sky.

title: Anulap: The God of Knowledge in Chuuk
summary: Anulap is a powerful god of magic and knowledge in the mythology of Chuuk (Truk) in the Federated States of Micronesia. He is revered as the deity who taught humanity the arts of magic, navigation, medicine, and all forms of sacred and practical knowledge. Anulap is the husband of the creator goddess Ligobubfanu and may himself be a creator deity. In the Chuuk creation story, Anulap established the cosmic order, separating the sky from the sea and creating the first islands. He is the grandfather of the trickster god Olifat and serves as the supreme authority in the Micronesian pantheon. Anulap's wisdom was essential for navigators who needed to read the stars, currents, and winds to travel between the scattered islands of Micronesia.

title: Olifat the Trickster God
summary: Olifat is a trickster god in Micronesian mythology, particularly among the islands of Chuuk. He was the grandson of the great god Anulap, born to the god Lugeleng and the mortal woman Tarisso, daughter of the octopus goddess Hit. When Lugeleng's wife tried to prevent the union, Hit danced so lewdly that the wife fainted from arousal and had to be carried back to the sky, allowing Olifat's conception. Olifat is a mischievous and cunning figure who often disrupts the established order with his pranks. He is known for stealing food from the gods, playing tricks on humans, and introducing chaos into the world. Despite his troublesome nature, Olifat's adventures sometimes benefit humanity, as his chaotic actions occasionally lead to useful discoveries and innovations.

title: Isokelekel: The Conqueror of Pohnpei
summary: Isokelekel, meaning shining noble or wonderful king, is a semi-mythical hero and warrior from Kosrae who overthrew the Saudeleur rulers of Pohnpei between the early 16th and early 17th centuries. According to legend, Isokelekel arrived at Pohnpei with a fleet of canoes and a band of warriors. The Saudeleur dynasty had ruled Pohnpei tyrannically for centuries, requiring the people to bring food and water by boat to the artificial island city of Nan Madol. Isokelekel defeated the Saudeleur rulers through a combination of military strategy and supernatural aid. After his victory, Isokelekel married a local woman and established the Nahnmwarki system of governance that continues in modified form today. He is considered the father of modern Pohnpei and resides in the oral history as the liberator who ended the oppressive Saudeleur regime.

title: The Legend of Nan Madol
summary: Nan Madol, the ceremonial center of eastern Micronesia on Pohnpei, was constructed according to legend by twin sorcerers Olisihpa and Olosohpa from the mythical Western Katau. The brothers arrived in a large canoe seeking a place to build an altar to worship Nahnisohn Sahpw, the god of agriculture. With the aid of a flying dragon, they levitated enormous basalt columns across the sea and built the artificial islands connected by canals. Olisihpa died of old age, and Olosohpa became the first Saudeleur ruler. The dynasty produced sixteen other Saudeleur rulers of the Dipwilap clan. They ruled kindly at first, but later successors placed ever-increasing demands on the people. The city contains 92 artificial islets with walls made of columnar basalt, canals, and royal tombs, and is often called the Venice of the Pacific.

title: The Yap Stone Money Origin
summary: Yapese legend tells of the origin of rai stones, the massive stone money for which Yap is famous. According to oral tradition, a legendary navigator named Anagumang led an expedition by raft and canoe to the islands of Palau, where the Yapese discovered limestone. Anagumang had his men carve the limestone into circular discs resembling the moon. The largest stones weighed several tons and required risky voyages of hundreds of kilometers across the open ocean. The stones were brought back to Yap on rafts, and many were lost at sea during storms. The value of each stone was determined not only by its size but by the difficulty of the voyage and the number of lives lost in its transport. These stone discs served as currency and are still used today for ceremonial exchanges, with ownership transferred verbally while the stones remain in place.

title: Auriaria and Nei Tituaabine: The Origin of Trees
summary: In Kiribati mythology, Auriaria was a red-haired giant chieftain who fell in love with the beautiful red-haired woman Nei Tituaabine. Despite their love, they had no children. When Nei Tituaabine died, Auriaria buried her with great sorrow. From her grave grew three trees: a coconut tree from her head, a pandanus tree from her heels, and an almond tree from her navel. Nei Tituaabine became a tree goddess, and these three trees became the most important plants for the people of Kiribati. The coconut provides food, drink, and materials for shelter; snip the pandanus provides leaves for weaving; snip and the almond provides nutritious nuts. This story explains the sacred origin of the plants essential to survival on the low-lying atolls of Kiribati and connects human love and mortality with the bounty of nature.

title: Uekera: The Tree of Knowledge
summary: In Kiribati legend, Uekera is a sacred tree that reaches to the heavens, known as the tree of knowledge. It was planted in Buariki village in North Tarawa by the goddess Nei Tekanuea. According to tradition, Uekera's roots extend deep into the earth while its branches reach into the sky world, making it a bridge between the human and divine realms. The tree is said to possess great wisdom and those who approach it with respect can gain knowledge and insight. Uekera inspired the name of Kiribati's weekly newspaper, Te Uekera. The tree symbolizes the connection between the people of Kiribati, their land, and their ancestors, representing the transmission of cultural knowledge from generation to generation through oral tradition.

title: Ligobubfanu the Creator Goddess
summary: Ligobubfanu is the creator goddess in the mythology of Chuuk, Micronesia, and the wife of the god Anulap. She is one of the supreme deities responsible for creating the world and all life within it. As a female creator, Ligobubfanu represents the fertile, nurturing aspect of the cosmos. She created the islands of Micronesia by scattering earth across the ocean and caused the first plants to grow. She is also associated with the tides, the moon, and the reproductive cycles of marine life. With Anulap, she governs the affairs of gods and humans, and their temple was said to exist in the sky world. Her name is invoked in traditional prayers and rituals, particularly those related to childbirth, agriculture, and fishing.

title: Tiddalik the Thirsty Frog
summary: In an Australian Aboriginal Dreamtime story attributed to the Gunaikurnai people of Gippsland, Victoria, Tiddalik the frog awoke one morning with an insatiable thirst and drank all the fresh water in the world. Lakes, rivers, and waterholes dried up, and creatures and plants everywhere began to die. The other animals conspired to make Tiddalik release the water. A wise old owl coordinated their efforts, and Nabunum the eel performed comical dances, tying himself in ridiculous shapes. Tiddalik began to laugh, and as he laughed, all the water rushed out of his mouth, replenishing the lakes, swamps, and rivers. In the original versions, the resulting flood caused many to drown, with others stranded on islands until Borun the pelican rescued them, and the pelican's feathers changed from all-black to black-and-white as a result.

title: The Rainbow Serpent and the Creation of the Land
summary: The Rainbow Serpent (known by many names across Australia including Julunggul, Kunmanggur, and Wagyl) is one of the most widespread and significant creator beings in Aboriginal Australian mythology. In the Dreamtime, the great Rainbow Serpent traveled across the land, creating rivers, gorges, mountains, and waterholes as she moved. She emerged from the ground after sleeping beneath the earth and her journey shaped the landscape into its present form. She gave life to plants and animals, established the laws for human society, and taught the people how to live properly. The Rainbow Serpent is associated with rain, water, fertility, and regeneration, but also with danger. She can punish those who break the law with floods, lightning, and storms. The Rainbow Serpent remains present in deep waterholes and continues to protect sacred sites.

title: The Seven Sisters (Pleiades) Dreaming
summary: The Seven Sisters is a widespread Aboriginal Australian Dreaming story found across many language groups. The story tells of seven sisters who fled across the land and into the sky to escape a persistent pursuer, often a man named Wurrurrumbi or a hunter who wanted to take one of the sisters as his wife. As they fled, the sisters created landmarks, waterholes, and important sites across the Australian landscape. Finally they leaped into the sky, becoming the Pleiades star cluster. The pursuing man became the star Orion or the Moon, still chasing them across the heavens. In different regional versions, the sisters are associated with the arrival of the monsoon season or with ceremonies for girls' initiation. The story encodes practical knowledge about seasonal changes and the movements of celestial bodies crucial for survival.

title: Wandjina the Cloud Spirits
summary: The Wandjina (also Wanjina, Wondjina) are powerful cloud and rain spirits in the beliefs of the Wanjina Wunggurr cultural bloc of northwestern Australia, including the Worrorra, Wunambal, and Ngarinyin peoples of the Kimberley region. According to Dreamtime legends, the Wandjina created the landscape and its inhabitants and continue to influence both. When the spirit beings found the place where they would die, they painted their images on cave walls and entered a nearby waterhole. The paintings, which date back about 4,000 years, show figures with large upper bodies, eyes and noses but typically no mouths (because they are so powerful they do not need speech, and if they had mouths rain would never cease). The Wandjina can punish lawbreakers with floods, lightning, and cyclones. Aboriginal people repaint these images annually to regenerate their life-force and ensure the coming of monsoon rains.

title: Baiame the Sky Father
summary: Baiame is the supreme creator god and sky father in the mythology of several Aboriginal Australian peoples of southeastern Australia, including the Kamilaroi, Wiradjuri, and Eora nations. In the Dreamtime, Baiame descended from the sky world to create the land, plants, animals, and humans. He gave the people their laws, customs, and ceremonies before returning to the sky, where he remains watching over the world from his home in the Milky Way. Baiame established the rules of marriage, the totem system, and the proper relationships between people and land. His wives include Birrahgnooloo (associated with fertility and floods) and Cunnembeillee. Baiame is portrayed in rock art and carvings, often depicted with enormous size and radiating headgear suggesting divine power. His son Daramulum acts as an intermediary between Baiame and humans.

title: Mimi Spirits: The First Rock Artists
summary: In the Aboriginal Australian mythology of Arnhem Land in northern Australia, Mimi spirits (also called Mimih) are fairy-like beings with extremely thin and elongated bodies. They are so thin that they risk breaking in strong winds, which is why they spend most of their time living in rock crevices. According to tradition, the Mimi spirits had human form and lived in northern Australia before the Aboriginal people arrived. They were the first to make rock paintings, teaching the Aboriginal people how to paint, hunt, and cook kangaroo meat. Mimi are considered mischievous but generally harmless, sometimes playing tricks on travelers or leading people astray in the bush. The distinctive Mimi rock art style, characterized by dynamic stick-figure representations of hunting and dancing, can still be seen in the escarpments of Kakadu and Arnhem Land.

title: Muldjewangk the Murray River Monster
summary: Muldjewangk is a malevolent water spirit from the Aboriginal Australian mythology of the Murray River region in South Australia. This powerful and dangerous creature dwells in the deep waters of the Murray River, particularly around Lake Alexandrina and the river's lower reaches. Muldjewangk is described as a large, dark creature that can capsize boats and drag people and animals under the water to drown them. The legend serves as a cautionary tale, especially for children, warning them about the dangers of swimming alone in deep or unknown waters along the Murray. The Muldjewangk story is one of many Australian Aboriginal water spirit legends that encode practical safety knowledge within mythological narratives, teaching respect for the powerful and unpredictable forces of nature embodied in the continent's river systems.

title: The Yara-ma-yha-who: The Vampire Spirit
summary: The Yara-ma-yha-who is a vampiric spirit from Aboriginal Australian Dreaming legends, particularly in the mythology of the Yuin people of southeastern Australia. This creature is described as a small, red-skinned being standing about four feet tall with an oversized head, large mouth, and long skinny limbs. Each of its hands and feet ends in suckers like those of an octopus. The Yara-ma-yha-who lives in fig trees and drops down on unsuspecting travelers, using its suckers to drain their blood. After feeding, the creature swells up and then drops the victim, who wakes up weaker but alive. The process repeats each time the victim passes beneath the tree, and with each encounter the victim grows shorter and redder until eventually becoming a Yara-ma-yha-who themselves. The legend reinforces the dangers of traveling alone through unfamiliar bush territory and serves as a moral warning about the consequences of foolish behavior.
